pattern: Collapsing every maximal run of non-special nodes into a single weighted blob turns a path-counting question on a tree into a running-total combinatorial sum taken around each special node.
hints: A path between two ordinary (non-prime) nodes contains exactly one prime only if that prime sits strictly between them on the tree path, so think about grouping the ordinary nodes that hang off each prime node into separate 'sides'.

First merge the ordinary nodes into connected components using only edges between two ordinary nodes (a prime node never joins a component); this gives you, for every ordinary node, the size of the blob it belongs to.

For each prime node, look at its direct neighbors: every non-prime neighbor contributes the size of its whole ordinary blob as one 'side'. Pairs of ordinary nodes on two different sides of the same prime path through exactly that prime, so accumulate a running total of side sizes and add running_total * next_side_size as you go (this is the classic 'multiply the new group against everything seen so far' trick). Separately, the prime node itself pairs with every ordinary node attached to it through any side (that path also crosses exactly one prime), so add the sum of all side sizes too. Two prime nodes never form a valid pair, because a path between two primes always has both of them on it.

edge_inputs.description: two nodes joined by one edge, the higher label is prime
star centered on a prime hub with several ordinary leaves
star centered on a non-prime hub with several ordinary and prime leaves mixed
two adjacent prime-labeled nodes must never form a counted pair with each other
a straight chain where several consecutive labels are prime in a row
several prime leaves all attach to the same merged non-prime hub blob, so each prime's side sum must reuse that shared blob size independently
a longer path graph of 60 nodes to exercise repeated prime crossings at moderate scale

stress.description: random tree grown by attaching each new node to a uniformly random earlier node (kept modest so the O(n^2) brute-force oracle used during authoring stays fast)
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 200))
    edges = []
    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        p = rng.randint(1, i - 1)
        edges.append([p, i])
    return [n, edges]

stress.description: random tree with shuffled labels so prime nodes land at arbitrary positions in the structure
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 200))
    nodes = list(range(1, n + 1))
    rng.shuffle(nodes)
    edges = []
    for i in range(1, n):
        j = rng.randint(0, i - 1)
        edges.append([nodes[i], nodes[j]])
    return [n, edges]
